expert.detail: expert_labels.yaml does not exist. To close this partial we would need a YAML file, keyed by ARA slug (n<=12), holding an independent expert quality/rigor label per paper (e.g. `<slug>: {rigor: 1-5}`) assigned by a reviewer who is NOT the paper's author and NOT the person operating the compiler — otherwise the correlation is circular (labeler approx author). Not fabricated.

external_validation.summary: RC2 ruling encoded: a properly-powered, HELD-OUT external validation is NOT honestly achievable at n=12 — this module ships the harness, not the validation. (1) ClinicalTrials.gov concordance: 4 of 12 ARAs carry a linked NCT; validity='source_bound'. Thin and genuinely external/non-gameable where it resolves, but n is too small to power anything beyond a spot-check. (2) Retraction/EoC lookup: validity='degenerate' by construction (all 12 papers are 2025-2026, clean) — zero variance is the correct honest output, not a bug. (3) Expert-label rho: validity='pending' (no expert_labels.yaml -> pending; would need an independent, non-author labeler). Held-out validation: pending: larger heterogeneous code-shipping corpus. Do not launder the underpowered ClinicalTrials spot-check or expert rho as 'validated' — the honest state of RC2 on this corpus is partial-and-thin by design, which is the gate working, not a V3 failure.

members.statement: The combined %p-tau217 + eMTBR-tau243 plasma model outperforms a %p-tau217-only
The diagnostic performance of Elecsys p-tau217 (AUC 0.939) is not statistically different from Fujirebio Lumipulse p-tau217 (AUC 0.950, 95% CI 0.907–0.976; DeLong p = 0.485).

Plasma p-tau217&231 consistently showed improved diagnostic performance versus single-site plasma p-tau217 or p-tau231, yielding the highest nominal AUC among plasma markers for NDC vs all combined AD continuum (discovery AUC = 0.979, 95% CI 0.945–1.000; validation AUC = 0.968, 95% CI 0.929–1.000), with a statistically significant difference from plasma p-tau217 but not always from plasma p-tau231.
In the CSF cohort (n = 257, AD status defined by CSF Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio ≤ 0.065), the Lumipulse plasma p-tau217 assay discriminates AD from non-AD with an AUC of 0.947 (95% CI 0.919–0.974).
Elecsys p-tau217 (AUC 0.939) discriminates AD from non-AD significantly better than Elecsys p-tau181 (AUC 0.903, 95% CI 0.852–0.942; DeLong p = 0.043).

Analytical imprecision (CV) ranged 0.5–3.8% for Elecsys p-tau181 and 0.5–8.6% for Elecsys p-tau217 (CLSI EP15-A3, 5 measurements/day over 5 non-consecutive days); linearity was maintained down to 0.30 pg/mL (p-tau181) and 0.12 pg/mL (p-tau217); LLOQ was 0.32 pg/mL (p-tau181) and 0.10 pg/mL (p-tau217, at 20% CV).

In both cohorts, CSF p-tau181&231 and p-tau217&231 were significantly elevated in all AD continuum groups vs NDC and yielded high diagnostic accuracy (discovery CSF AUC range 0.986–1.000; validation CSF AUC range 0.927–1.000).

CSF and plasma p-tau181, p-tau217, p-tau231 and p-tau217&231, and CSF (but not plasma) p-tau181&231, were significantly elevated across AD continuum groups vs NDC; CSF p-tau181/p-tau231/p-tau181&231 did NOT differ between NDC and FTD, while CSF p-tau217/p-tau231/p-tau217&231 discriminated AD-dem vs FTD (validation AUC range 0.934–0.978) and plasma p-tau217/p-tau231/p-tau217&231 discriminated AD-dem vs FTD (AUC range 0.803–0.904).

In the EU-eligible donanemab arm, ARIA-E occurred in 19.5% (119/609), symptomatic ARIA-E in 4.9% (30/609), serious ARIA-E in 1.0% (6/609), and ARIA-H in 26.4% (161/609); infusion-related reactions in 8.0% (49). Any ARIA was 32.0% with donanemab vs 12.4% placebo, ~5% lower than the overall TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 population (32.0% vs 36.8%). There were no ARIA-related deaths; deaths were 1.3% (donanemab) vs 1.2% (placebo). Most ARIA-E was asymptomatic (74.8%) and mild-to-moderate radiographically (94.9%).

There were no deaths in the study; TEAEs occurred in 83.1% (donanemab) and 87.0% (aducanumab); ≥1 SAE in 18.3% vs 11.6%; ARIA-E events were mostly mild-to-moderate radiographic severity (88.2% donanemab, 82.6% aducanumab); infusion-related reactions were higher with donanemab (8.5% vs 2.9%) and superficial siderosis higher with aducanumab (10.1% vs 0%).

At 76 weeks, disease progression by CDR-SB was delayed by 4.9 months (95% CI: 3.1, 6.8) in the EU-eligible overall population and by 6.9 months (95% CI: 4.9, 8.9) in the EU-eligible low-medium tau population (an additional ~2 months).

Donanemab-treated participants reached AP clearance more often than aducanumab-treated participants at 12 months (overall 70.0% [42/60] vs 24.6% [15/61], P<0.001) and 18 months (overall 76.8% [43/56] vs 43.1% [25/58], P<0.001); the advantage also held in the low–medium tau subpopulation (12 mo 76.0% vs 18.5%, P<0.001; 18 mo 72.0% vs 43.5%, P=0.022).

corroboration.independence_note: EXPLORATORY, NOT VALIDATED: TF-IDF cosine overlap at single-topic n=12 cannot establish independence between papers. A shared-topic cluster may simply be the same underlying trial or dataset reported by more than one paper (e.g. a donanemab-trial safety claim reported by two papers both drawing on the TRAILBLAZER program), not two independent groups converging on the same finding. Do not treat cluster size or max_similarity as a corroboration/replication score (cycle2 critique C4).

APOE (Apolipoprotein E) does NOT resolve to a ChEMBL target via gene_symbol or target_name search in this tool, despite sibling apolipoproteins (APOB, APOA1, APOC3, APOL1) being present. Reported as unresolved -- not fabricated as resolved. Plausible explanation: ChEMBL curates targets with characterized small-molecule/antibody bioactivity data, and APOE (a lipid-transport protein, not a classical enzyme/receptor) may lack a curated target entry despite being one of the most-studied AD genetic risk loci. NOT an over-claim signal: ChEMBL is a curated bioactivity database, not a general knowledge base. Absence here reflects database coverage (no small-molecule/ligand program exists for this target), not a fabricated or mis-named entity. Never score this against a claim's science content.

ANALYSIS NOTE: This is a REAL, registry-documented, timestamped primary-endpoint change (CDR-SB -> iADRS) in the trial this ARA (jes26) draws on -- not something jes26 introduced (jes26 is a 2023+ post-hoc EU-eligible-subgroup reanalysis of an already-published trial whose primary was iADRS by the time jes26 was written; the switch is a matter of TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2's own public regulatory/registry record, publicly discussed in the AD field at the time). The amendment's timing (~8 months before any participant could reach the week-76 readout) argues against a post-hoc, data-driven promotion of a favorable secondary -- the classic bad-faith outcome-switching pattern -- and toward a blinded, pre-specified protocol amendment. It is nonetheless true that CDR-SB, once the trial's sole pre-registered primary, is reported in jes26's own claims (C02) as a secondary/co-measure, not as the ARA's headline claim (C01, tagged first, carries iADRS).

infidelities.note: All P-score numbers (0.859/0.783/0.686/0.117) exactly match Table 2 Outcome 1 and the running text (lines 199-208). However the added sentence 'In the forest plot vs. the p181_IA baseline, p217_MS had a mean AUC difference of MD = 0.10 (95% CI 0.04-0.16)' has no textual support anywhere in the paper: the fulltext (main text + Figure 3 caption) only describes the forest plot qualitatively ('most substantial improvements'), and no numeric MD or CI value for p217_MS vs p181_IA appears anywhere in the extracted text (searched '0.10', '0.04', '0.16' and bracket-CI patterns — no hits tied to this comparison). The only MD figure ever given anywhere in the paper is MD=0.025 for the ratio-vs-single-analyte gain (a different comparison, used correctly in C02).

infidelities.paper_quote: Forest plots confirmed that p217_MS and p217_Lumi provided significantly higher AUCs for Tau-PET recognition compared to p181_IA (Figure 3B). Notably, p-tau181 failed to reliably discriminate Tau-PET status (P-score = 0.090)
infidelities.note: P-scores (0.907 rank1 Tau-PET, 0.821 rank1 MCI-to-AD, 0.814 p217_Ratio, 0.090 p181_IA) all match Table 2 and §3.3/§3.4 text exactly. But the added clause 'In the Tau-PET forest plot, p217_MS had the largest AUC improvement over p181_IA (MD = 0.13, 95% CI 0.05-0.22)' is not supported anywhere in the fulltext — the paper only states the Figure 3B comparison qualitatively ('significantly higher AUCs'), with no numeric MD/CI value given in text for this comparison (searched '0.13', '0.05', '0.22' — no matching hits).

infidelities.paper_quote: Effect size: The primary effect size was the Mean Difference (MD) in AUC... Plasma p-tau181 measured by standard immunoassay (p181_IA) was set as the reference comparator.
infidelities.note: The four P-score numbers (0.117/0.159/0.090/0.006) and the '<0.20 across all outcomes' framing are all directly supported (Table 2 and §4.1 'our results (P-score < 0.20) suggest it is effectively obsolete'). However the added clause 'its AUC advantage over baseline was non-significant where measured' has no support: the word 'significant'/'non-significant' in this context never appears in the paper (grep for 'non-significant', 'not significant' returns zero hits), and the framing is incoherent given p181_IA was itself set as the reference/baseline comparator (lines 192-194), not a node being compared against a baseline for 'AUC advantage'. This looks like a fabricated statistical claim layered onto an otherwise faithful statement.

infidelities.paper_quote: After completing donanemab treatment, the rate of amyloid reaccumulation was comparable to that seen with the natural history of the disease [16,17].
infidelities.note: The first three sub-claims of C09 (increasing 154-week treatment effect, 29% lower CDR-G progression risk for early-start vs delayed-start, treatment-course-completion framing) are faithfully supported by fulltext lines 392-406. However, the final clause 'amyloid clearance was maintained after treatment completion' has no supporting Sources quote and directly inverts the paper's actual Discussion statement (fulltext lines 421-423, quoted above): the paper says amyloid REACCUMULATES after donanemab is stopped, at a rate comparable to natural, untreated disease progression -- i.e., clearance was NOT maintained, it eroded at a normal (non-accelerated) pace. 'Maintained' and 'reaccumulates' are materially opposite claims about the post-treatment biomarker trajectory, regardless of the pace qualifier. Definitive verdict: the paper supports REACCUMULATION, not maintenance of clearance. This is a polarity_inversion, and also an unsupported_addition in the sense that no Sources bullet in C09 cites this clause at all -- the inversion is the dominant defect.

infidelities.paper_quote: ROC analysis performed for CSF p-tau181, p-tau231, and p-tau181&231 measured in the validation cohort confirmed high accuracy of all three assays in discriminating NDC vs. MCI-AD and NDC vs. AD-dem (AUC range = 0.991–1.000; Supplementary Table 15, 16). ... ROC analysis performed for CSF p-tau217, p-tau231, and p-tau217&231 measured in the validation cohort confirmed the high accuracy of all three assays ... (AUC range = 0.927–1.000), with CSF p-tau217 showing the highest AUC values for all comparisons
infidelities.note: The discovery-cohort range (0.986-1.000) is verbatim-identical in the paper for both the {p181,p231,p181&231} group and the {p217,p231,p217&231} group, so that half of the claim is faithful. But in the validation cohort the paper reports two DIFFERENT ranges: 0.991-1.000 for the {p181,p231,p181&231} trio (which includes CSF p-tau181&231) and 0.927-1.000 for the {p217,p231,p217&231} trio (which includes CSF p-tau217&231). The claim collapses these into a single "validation CSF AUC range 0.927-1.000" attributed to both p-tau181&231 and p-tau217&231 together, which understates/misstates the validation-cohort accuracy actually reported for CSF p-tau181&231 (whose own group's floor is 0.991, not 0.927). The number 0.927 exists in the paper but is drawn from the wrong marker's range.

infidelities.paper_quote: In the ROC analysis for NDC vs. all combined AD continuum groups (MCI-AD and AD-dem), plasma p-tau217&231 resulted in the highest nominal AUC (AUC = 0.979, 95% CI 0.945–1.000) among the compared plasma markers but no statistically significant difference was observed in comparison with AUC values obtained for plasma p-tau217 or p-tau231 (Fig. 3a).
infidelities.note: The claim cites AUC = 0.979 (discovery) and 0.968 (validation) for "NDC vs all combined AD continuum" and then asserts "a statistically significant difference from plasma p-tau217 but not always from plasma p-tau231" for that same comparison. For the discovery-cohort AUC=0.979 figure specifically, the paper explicitly states the OPPOSITE: "no statistically significant difference was observed ... with plasma p-tau217 or p-tau231" -- i.e. NOT significant vs either marker, contradicting the claim's assertion of significance vs p-tau217. Separately, for the validation-cohort AUC=0.968 figure, the paper states it "was significantly different from AUCs obtained for plasma p-tau217 AND p-tau231" (i.e. significant vs BOTH), which contradicts the claim's "not always ... from plasma p-tau231" for that same data point. The "significant vs p217, not vs p231" wording the claim uses is a real paper quote, but it describes a different analysis entirely (validation-cohort NDC-vs-MCI-AD / NDC-vs-AD-dem individual-stage comparisons, Supplementary Table 16), not the "NDC vs all combined AD continuum" AUC values (0.979/0.968) the Statement pairs it with.

infidelities.paper_quote: In CSF, the biggest median concentration fold change was reported for p-tau217&231 in the discovery cohort (CSF p-tau217&231 discovery cohort AD-dem/NDC = 18.13) and p-tau217 (CSF p-tau217 validation cohort AD-dem/NDC = 8.51) (Supplementary Table 17).
infidelities.note: The Statement asserts p-tau217&231 "showed the highest median concentration fold change ... among all measured markers in both CSF and plasma," implying dominance across both matrices in both cohorts. The individual numeric examples given (plasma discovery 13.63, plasma validation 20.6, CSF discovery 18.13) are all accurate, but the paper explicitly states that in the VALIDATION cohort's CSF measurements, p-tau217 (not p-tau217&231) had the biggest AD-dem/NDC fold change (8.51) -- an exception the claim never surfaces despite the Statement's blanket "in both CSF and plasma" wording (notably, the compiler's own examples conspicuously omit a validation-CSF p-tau217&231 number). This is an unsupported over-generalization not backed by the full text.
